chain_name: Yeast autolysis during tirage lees contact → mannoprotein and polysaccharide release → texture and stability

starting_factor.description: After secondary fermentation in bottle (tirage), Champagne spends months or years in contact with the dead yeast cells (lees) that performed the fermentation.

intermediate_steps.description: Dead yeast cells undergo autolysis — their own enzymes break down the cell wall and intracellular components. This is a slow process accelerated by higher temperature and longer contact time. It requires a minimum of 15 months for NV Champagne and 36 months for Vintage under AOC regulations.

Autolysis releases mannoproteins (glycoproteins) and polysaccharides from the yeast cell wall into the wine. These large, viscous macromolecules coat the CO₂ bubbles, increasing their stability and reducing the rate at which they coalesce and escape — creating finer, more persistent, smaller bubbles.

Mannoproteins and polysaccharides also contribute directly to palate weight and texture — a creamier, rounder mouthfeel and a perception of greater body even in a bone-dry wine. They also improve tartrate stability by inhibiting tartrate crystallisation.

final_outcome.description: Finer, more persistent bubbles; creamier texture; improved tartrate stability; and the foundation for autolytic aromatic character. The longer the tirage contact, the more pronounced these effects — though there is a practical maximum beyond which the wine may tire.

examples.description: Minimum legal autolysis time produces some texture and bubble refinement. Most quality-focused NV houses age significantly longer (2–4 years) to develop more pronounced autolytic character and bubble fineness.

Extended tirage produces significantly increased mannoprotein content. Premier Cru and Grand Cru wines often spend 5–8 years on lees, creating exceptionally fine, long-lived bubbles and a distinctive richness of texture.

related_exam_questions: What is autolysis and how does it affect the style of Champagne?
Why does extended lees contact improve the quality of Champagne's bubbles?
What is the minimum lees ageing requirement for NV and Vintage Champagne?
distinction_note: A Distinction answer names mannoproteins and polysaccharides specifically, explains their role in bubble stability (coating CO₂ bubbles to slow coalescence) as distinct from their role in texture, and distinguishes the minimum legal requirement from typical quality-house practice.
